sections.heading: Background: The Radical Reformation
sections.html:
Background: The Radical Reformation

The Schleitheim Confession (February 24, 1527) emerged from the most radical wing of the sixteenth-century Reformation — a movement that rejected not only the Roman Catholic sacramental system but also the magisterial Reformers' willingness to retain a territorial, state-supported church. To understand Schleitheim, one must understand the Zurich Anabaptist circle and the urgent pressures that brought it into existence.

The Swiss Anabaptist movement was born in Zurich in January 1525. Conrad Grebel (c. 1498–1526), Felix Manz (c. 1498–1527), and a circle of humanist students had initially worked alongside Ulrich Zwingli in his reformation of Zurich. But they grew increasingly dissatisfied with Zwingli's pace and, above all, with his deference to the Zurich city council on matters of church reform. The decisive break came over the question of infant baptism. Grebel, Manz, and their companions concluded from their study of the New Testament that baptism belongs exclusively to those who consciously confess faith and receive it as a sign of their discipleship — not to infants who can make no such confession.

On January 21, 1525, in the home of Felix Manz in Zurich, the first recorded rebaptism of the Reformation era took place: Georg Blaurock asked Conrad Grebel to baptize him upon his personal confession of faith, and Blaurock then baptized the others present. Because they were re-baptizing already-baptized individuals (from Rome's standpoint), their opponents called them Wiedertäufer — Anabaptists, or "re-baptizers" — a name that carried the death penalty under ancient Roman law. The Zurich council promptly banned the practice, and Felix Manz was drowned in the Limmat River on January 5, 1527 — the first Anabaptist martyr at Protestant hands, executed by the very method that mocked his convictions about water baptism.

By early 1527, the young Anabaptist communities in southern Germany and Switzerland were under severe persecution from both Catholic and Protestant authorities, and they were also experiencing internal fragmentation. Itinerant leaders held conflicting positions on the sword, the oath, and how to relate to established civil society. The community desperately needed a statement of its core convictions to provide coherence and a basis for fellowship among scattered congregations.

Into this crisis stepped Michael Sattler (c. 1490–1527), a former Benedictine prior of St. Peter's monastery near Freiburg who had left monastic life, married a former Beguine named Margaretha, and joined the Anabaptist movement around 1525. Sattler possessed both theological training and pastoral gifts. He is universally credited as the primary author of the seven articles that the gathering at Schleitheim (in the canton of Schaffhausen, near the Swiss-German border) ratified on February 24, 1527.

The meeting sought to address the divisions and define the boundaries of true Christian community as the Anabaptists understood it. The seven articles that resulted are not a comprehensive systematic theology — they are a brotherly union (brüderliche Vereinigung), a practical covenant specifying the marks of the gathered church that would separate it from "the world, the Pope, Luther, Zwingli, and all the rest." The document is addressed to "the brothers and sisters who have been scattered here and there," written in the intimate, urgent language of a persecuted community forming its identity under pressure.

Three months after Schleitheim, Michael Sattler was arrested at Horb. At his trial before the Austrian Habsburg authorities, he was accused of heresy, sedition, and undermining the war against the Turks (because he had stated Christians should not fight). On May 20, 1527, he was burned at the stake at Rottenburg am Neckar — his tongue cut out, his flesh torn with red-hot tongs before the fire was lit, and his ashes scattered so that no grave could become a shrine. His wife Margaretha was drowned eight days later. Their martyrdom, far from suppressing the movement, gave the Schleitheim articles an apostolic authority they might never have achieved otherwise. (Acts 5:29; Matt. 28:19)

sections.heading: The Seven Articles
sections.html:
The Seven Articles

The Schleitheim Confession consists of seven articles (Artikel), each addressing a specific point of practice and ecclesiology. They do not follow the pattern of later Reformed confessions (which typically work through loci from God to eschatology); instead they are almost entirely ecclesiological and ethical — defining the shape of the gathered, separated community of believers.

Article 1 — Baptism. The first article establishes believers' baptism as the only legitimate baptism. It shall be given to "all those who have learned repentance and amendment of life, and who believe truly that their sins are taken away by Christ, and to all those who walk in the resurrection of Jesus Christ." Infant baptism is explicitly excluded: "This excludes all infant baptism, the highest and chief abomination of the Pope." The positive basis is the Great Commission, in which instruction in discipleship precedes the act of baptism: the pattern is hear, believe, repent, then be baptized. (Matt. 28:19; Acts 2:38)

Article 2 — The Ban. The second article establishes the practice of church discipline — the ban (Bann) — as the mechanism by which the purity of the gathered community is maintained. Any baptized member who "slips" into sin shall be warned twice in private; if they do not amend, they shall be banned before the whole congregation. The ban was to operate before the Lord's Supper, to preserve the integrity of the table. Critically, Schleitheim insists that the ban applies even "before the breaking of bread" — the exclusion must precede communion, not follow it as an afterthought. The pastoral intention is explicitly restorative: "that God may be glorified and we not become partakers of their sins." (Matt. 18:15–17; 1 Cor. 5:11)

Article 3 — Breaking of Bread. The Lord's Supper is for baptized believers only, those who have been united to Christ and to one another through faith and the covenant of baptism. The article explicitly rejects the Roman Mass: "all the papal and anti-papal works of masses and communion" are excluded. The Supper is an act of remembrance and corporate proclamation of Christ's death among those who are already members of the body. Only those who have been baptized upon their confession of faith may participate. (1 Cor. 11:23–26)

Article 4 — Separation from the World. This article, the most sweeping in scope, commands a comprehensive separation from "the abomination" — from all Catholic and Protestant establishments alike. The community must separate from "the devilish weapons of force — such as sword, armor and the like, and all their use either for friends or against enemies." Members may not participate in civil government, attend "taverns," or be yoked with unbelievers in any way. The article is grounded in the Pauline call to holiness: "Come out from among them and be ye separate, saith the Lord, and touch not the unclean thing." The separation is total — ecclesial, social, and moral. (2 Cor. 6:17)

Article 5 — Shepherds. The congregational pastor (Hirt, shepherd) is to be a man of good repute, "one who has a good report of those who are outside the faith." He is to be called and supported by the local congregation — "the congregation that has elected him shall also support him, wherein he has need." This is a thoroughgoing congregational polity: the church calls its own minister and the church funds its own minister. There is no hierarchy above the local assembly, no bishop of diocesan authority, no state salary. If a shepherd is expelled or martyred, another shall be ordained in his place at once. (1 Cor. 9:14)

Article 6 — The Sword. Article Six is the most theologically elaborated article in the document and the one with the longest-reaching historical consequences. The sword (Schwert) — coercive, lethal force — belongs to the ordering of the world outside the perfection of Christ. God has ordained the magistrate for the punishment of the wicked and the protection of the good, but this office lies outside the Christian's calling. A Christian may not serve as a magistrate, pronounce a death sentence, or take up arms in warfare, because "the government's magistracy is according to the flesh, but the Christians' is according to the Spirit." When Christ was asked to judge between brothers over an inheritance, he refused (Luke 12:14), and so too the Christian must refuse the sword's role. The article is not anarchist — it acknowledges the magistrate's legitimate function in the unredeemed world — but it draws a sharp line between Christ's kingdom and the kingdoms of this age. (Matt. 5:38–39; Rom. 13:4)

Article 7 — The Oath. The final article prohibits the swearing of oaths of any kind — by heaven, earth, sword, head, or anything else. This follows the plain command of the Sermon on the Mount: "But I say to you, Do not swear at all... Let what you say be simply 'Yes' or 'No'; anything more than this comes from evil." The oath was deeply embedded in the structures of civil and feudal society (loyalty oaths, court oaths, guild oaths); to refuse it was to stand outside those structures entirely. The Anabaptists insisted that a Christian's word must always be perfectly reliable — there is no special category of speech that demands reinforcement by an oath; all speech should be oath-quality truth. (Matt. 5:34–37)

sections.heading: Significance and Legacy
sections.html:
Significance and Legacy

The Schleitheim Confession occupies a unique position in the history of Christian doctrine. It is the first Protestant confession of the free-church tradition — the tradition that insists that the church is constituted not by territorial geography, political establishment, or infant enrollment, but by the voluntary, conscious covenant of regenerate believers gathered in the name of Christ. In this, Schleitheim set the terms of an ecclesiological debate that has continued for five centuries.

Its theological contributions were simultaneously radical and deeply traditional. In insisting on believers' baptism, the Anabaptists were not being novel — they argued from the New Testament pattern itself: the disciples heard the gospel, believed, repented, and then were baptized (Acts 2:41). The 3,000 baptized at Pentecost were those who "received his word." What was radical was the conclusion: that the entire Christendom edifice built on the presumption that the Christian population equals the baptized population was a fundamental error. The church is gathered, not given; it is constituted by the Spirit's work, not by civil registration.

The Schleitheim articles were foundational for the streams of Christian community that trace their lineage to the Zurich Anabaptists. The Mennonite tradition, organized around the pastoral legacy of Menno Simons (1496–1561), took the Schleitheim Confession as a basic reference point for its identity, though the more authoritative Mennonite confession would be the Dordrecht Confession of 1632. The Amish, who separated from the Swiss Mennonites in 1693, maintained the strictest possible reading of the Schleitheim articles on the ban and separation. The Swiss Brethren (the direct heirs of the Zurich circle) carried the same tradition through the valleys of Switzerland and into the Palatinate.

More broadly, the Schleitheim Confession represents the first systematic articulation of four principles that would become defining marks of what historians call the "left wing" or "Radical" Reformation:

  • Believers' baptism — conversion and conscious faith precede and ground the baptismal act.
  • Congregational discipline — the church has both the right and the duty to maintain moral boundaries among its members through the ban.
  • Pacifism — the sword is incompatible with the way of Christ; the Christian community does not coerce, wage war, or execute.
  • Separation of church and state — the congregation is self-governing, self-funded, and independent of civil authority in all matters of worship, membership, and discipline.

These principles, worked out under the pressure of persecution and martyrdom, would eventually (through quite different channels — English Separatists, Baptists, and Quakers) become pillars of the broader Western tradition of religious liberty. The irony is rich: those who were drowned, burned, and beheaded for their ecclesiology became, across the centuries, progenitors of the very concept of freedom of conscience that now governs the liberal democratic world.

The vision of the church that undergirds Schleitheim is directly drawn from the New Testament picture of gathered communities of disciples: "For where two or three are gathered in my name, there am I among them" (Matt. 18:20). The kingdom Jesus proclaimed is not of this world (John 18:36), and therefore the church that embodies that kingdom cannot be identified with any state, any nation, or any coercive structure. That conviction — which cost Michael and Margaretha Sattler their lives — remains the beating heart of the free-church tradition to this day. (Acts 2:41; John 18:36)
